title: Rebate reform ‘to hit elderly poor hardest’
full_text:
A coalition of medical, hospital and seniors groups is urging Anthony Albanese to reconsider his budget move to scrap the higher private health insurance rebate for over-65s, saying more than three million older Australians will be affected, including many on modest incomes.
The Australian Medical Association, the Australian Private Hospitals Association and National Seniors Australia are among those who have jointly written to the Prime Minister and Health Minister Mark Butler asking for further consultation before the change becomes law.
The letter argues the reform “will fall hardest on the older Australians least able to absorb it, will shift substantial cost to public hospitals already operating at capacity, and will compound an emerging viability crisis in private hospitals, particularly in regional Australia”.
And it says aged-care residents could face delays in elective surgery for joint replacements or cataracts, leading to “materially worse” health and mortality outcomes. Other signatories include Private Healthcare Australia and Members Health Fund Alliance, both representing health insurers, Catholic Health Australia, Patients Australia and the Medical Technology Association of Australia. The letter is unusual in that private hospitals and private health insurers are often in fierce conflict over pricing contracts.
Mr Butler flagged the change to private health insurance rebates in a National Press Club speech in April ahead of the budget, saying it was “not fair” that older Australians paid less for their insurance than younger generations with the same incomes.
He said the estimated $3bn in budget savings from the change would be spent on new aged-care packages, including 5000 more beds a year and a change to the Support at Home program to ensure personal services like assisted showering are covered.
At present, Australians with eligible health cover receive an income-tested rebate on hospital and general outpatient products. Families on the basic tier earning less than $202,000 a year (or singles earning less than $101,000) will receive a 24 per cent rebate if they are aged 65 or under, 28 per cent if they are aged 65-69 and 32 per cent for those over 70.
In tier two, singles earning $118-000-$158,000 or families on $236,000-$316,000 receive an 8 per cent rebate, rising to 12 per cent for 65 to 69-year-olds and 16 per cent for those over 70. Those earning more receive no rebate.
The government’s planned change would remove these higher rebates for over-65s. Since Mr Butler’s announcement a range of stakeholders have expressed serious concern about the impact on the hospital sector and on older Australians who are not rich being penalised.
This new letter, a show of force from across the health sector, flags that low-income older Australians who are more price-responsive than others and are more likely to be affected.
The government has said the average cost increase for over-65s would be between $226 and $255 a year, but the private health insurers say the rebate cut will contribute to estimated premium increases of up to $1600 for a couple in their 70s on gold cover.
Mr Butler said the government expected about 44,000 older Australians to drop out of PHI as a result of the changes, but the letter warns the government hasn’t considered the impact of people downgrading their cover.
“The policy treats all over-65s identically (but) the cohort the government is least intending to displace (pensioners and low-income retirees) is the cohort most likely to drop or downgrade their cover,” it says.
“The (Health) department’s impact analysis acknowledges that downgrade behaviour, specifically patients moving from gold to silver or adding exclusions, was not included in the modelling, and this could materially worsen the impact on public hospitals.
“Downgrades typically remove cover for cataracts, joint replacement and mental health, which are the services most needed by the 65+ cohort.” This increased demand on public hospitals would fall heavily on the states, who carry around 55 per cent of the additional hospital activity under state-federal arrangements, “while receiving none of the rebate saving”.
A spokeswoman for Mr Butler said the additional rebate being provided to over-65s “is simply not the best way to spend precious taxpayer dollars on behalf of older Australians when we need to do so much heavy lifting in aged care”. “While some people may choose to leave private health insurance because of this change, we project this would be around 0.4 per cent,” she said.
